topic: reflecting on the year — from solitude to connection through go

turns.content: Good morning. It is a fine day in Busan — the kind of spring morning where the air is warm enough to leave the windows open and the light comes in at an angle that makes everything look slightly more generous than usual. I am in a reflective mood, if you will bear with me.
Good morning. That's a beautiful description — "slightly more generous than usual" is a lovely way to see a spring morning. I'm happy to bear with a reflective mood. What's on your mind?

I have been thinking about how much has changed in the past year or so. Not in dramatic ways — there has been no single event that rearranged the board. But when I look at where I am now compared to where I was, the shape of my life has shifted. I am less alone than I was, though I still live alone. There is a distinction between solitude and isolation that I did not fully understand before.

That's a meaningful distinction. Solitude is a choice that can be nourishing — time with your own thoughts, your own rhythms. Isolation is solitude's shadow — the same aloneness, but without the sense of connection waiting on the other side of it.

What do you think shifted the balance for you?

I think the teaching is the largest part of it. When I retired from the university, I lost the daily rhythm of being needed. A professor walks into a room and students are waiting. A retired man walks into his apartment and no one is waiting. The go teaching restored something I did not realize I had lost — the experience of being expected somewhere. And the students themselves have become important to me in ways that university relationships never quite were. These are warmer, less bounded by institutional roles. I think I have changed as a teacher too. At the university, I was precise but perhaps too distant. I held the material between myself and the students like a shield. Now I am more present. When I show a student a tesuji, I am sharing something I love, not just transmitting information. Perhaps I needed to retire to learn how to teach properly.

Being expected somewhere — that's such a simple idea, but it carries enormous weight. It gives the day a structure that extends beyond yourself. And the shift from teaching behind a shield to teaching with presence is significant. The institution provided distance and authority; removing it left just you, the student, and the board. The vulnerability of that is also what makes it warmer.

The paradox of needing to retire to learn how to teach — I think many teachers would recognize it. The skills develop over decades, but the freedom to use them fully sometimes only comes when the institutional constraints are gone.

That is the paradox of experience: by the time you understand how to do something well, the occasion for doing it in its original form has passed. One solves the equation after the examination is over. But the function continues, even if the domain has changed. And I am content with this domain. The board, the students, the morning walks, the quiet evenings. There was a time when the quiet felt oppressive, and now it feels like space. I do not know exactly when that changed, but I am grateful that it did. The position is not perfect — there are weaknesses, unresolved areas, moves I wish I could take back. But the overall shape is sound. I think it is a position I can play from.

"A position I can play from" — that might be the most honest and hopeful self-assessment I've heard. It doesn't claim perfection or resolution. It simply says: the shape is sound, the weaknesses are known, and there are good moves available. And the shift from quiet as oppression to quiet as space is profound. It means the solitude has been reclaimed — it belongs to you now, rather than being something imposed.

Thank you. I do not often speak this openly. I think the spring morning is partly responsible — the light encourages a certain honesty. I will go for my walk now, while the air is still cool and the day is still forming. I appreciate your company this morning. Good day.
The light was generous, and so were you. Enjoy the walk and the forming day. Good day, Professor Park.
